text:
Official Communication: Relations between Local Government and Society in Poland, 16th – 18th centuries monograph release presentation This publication covers one of the most important periods in the evolution of communication methods used by Polish state authorities in relation to civic society. The research material consists of selected Polish language sources from the administration offices of towns from 16th-18th century Upper Silesia, including: Tarnowskie Góry, Woźniki, Chorzów, Mysłowice and Opole. According to available data, in this period the main language used by the offices in that region was Polish. Used in a framework of European municipal law this phenomenon shows the evolution of the official Polish language used in institutional communication in state administration offices in Poland and other countries.

While traditional historical linguistics preoccupies itself with the recounting of historical events, this work represents a more complex approach to linguistically analyzing written sources from bygone eras. By bringing in a new focus to the analysis of medieval texts, the author ascertains two communication fields within that time period: chancellery discourse and court discourse. Thus chancellery discourse dealt with matters like deeds of sale, marital agreements, last wills etc. within the framework of providing legal guarantees to the population. The main purpose of court discourse, on the other hand, was to settle the claims of disputing parties, to keep society safe and to prepare complaints that could be used in courts in case of a controversy arising over a trial. The author of the work examines in great detail the pragmatics of the sources, the formal features of the texts as well as the development of genres that constituted legal procedures at that time. Accordingly, this work explores the pioneering role of medieval legal proceedings and their consequences for modern bureaucratic and procedural law language.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
